title: Property Rights to Client Relationships and Financial Advisor Incentives
abstract: We study the effect of a change in property rights on employee behavior in the financial advice industry. Our identification comes from staggered firm-level entry into theProtocol for Broker Recruiting, which waived nonsolicitation clauses for advisor transitions among member firms, effectively transferring ownership of client relationships from the firm to the advisor. After the shock, advisors appear to tend to client relationships more by investing in client-facing industry licenses, shifting to fee-based advising, and reducing customer complaints. Our findings support property rights based investment theories of the firm and document offsetting costs to restricting labor mobility.

article.introduction:
Financial advisors, and the firms theywork for, play a key role in facilitating household access to financial markets. By getting to know their customer and building trust, an advisor can encourage the client to take appropriate risk (Gennaioli, Shleifer, and Vishny (2015)) and can identify the best-suited financial products for the client (Inderst and Ottaviani (2012)). Though intangible, client relationships can be thought as a productive asset for an advisor. The returns to the advisor of investing in such client relationships, however, are realized over a long period and require significant upfront investment. Rather than investing such effort, advisors may be tempted to instead pursue short-term gains through commissions, kickbacks, or other forms of opportunism vis-a-vis unsophisticated clients.1Property rights based models of the firm suggest that whether ownership of a productive asset, such as a client relationship, lies with the firm or the employee (i.e., the individual financial advisor) can affect the employee's willingness to invest in the productive asset due to the threat of hold-up (Williamson (1979), Grossman and Hart (1986), Hart and Moore (1990)).
Although clients may come to view the advisor as the face of the client relationship, legally their account is affiliated with the advisory firm. Typical employment agreements in the industry include nonsolicitation clauses that prohibit a departing advisor from contacting their clients and trying to move them to their new firm. Talented advisors who are able to gain the trust of their wealthy clients and attract new assets are in high demand in the competitive advisory industry. Not surprisingly, recruiting among firms is commonplace. An advisor's book of business, and the future revenue it generates, comprises a significant fraction of her value. As such, a departing advisor often gathers client information before leaving in an effort to bring clients with them. In response, incumbent firms have often sought temporary restraining orders against the departing advisor and sued for breach of employment contract in the hope of retaining clients. Frequent advisor poaching between firms thus led to costly, public legal battles. In addition, when Congress passed the Gramm-Leach-Bliley Act in 1999, which protects client financial data such as contact and account information under Regulation S-P, lawyers for large wirehouse firms began to fear that advisor recruiting, which often includes acquiring personal client information, increased their firms' legal risk.2
To anticipate this risk, on August 19, 2004, three of the largest New York brokerage firms (Smith Barney, UBS, and Merrill Lynch) privately entered intoThe Protocol for Broker Recruiting(The Protocol). The Protocol allowed departing advisors to retain a limited set of client data and to solicit their clients when moving to other Protocol member firms without fear of legal recourse, while advisors who left for non-Protocol firms did not enjoy such protections. Over the next decade, more than 1,500 firms nationwide joined the agreement. Beyond reducing legal liability during advisor transitions, the Protocol effectively shifted property rights to client relationships from the firm to the advisor by waiving nonsolicitation agreements within Protocol transitions. Once covered by Protocol, advisors can retain the client relationship. This allows the advisor to recoup investments made in such relationships. Moreover, by increasing her bargaining power vis-a-vis the firm, it allows her to capture more of the surplus created from investment in client relationships.3A further implication is that the advisor bears the cost of opportunistic behavior that damages the value of the trust between advisor and client. Investing in client relationships thus becomes more in line with the advisor's interest.
In this paper, we exploit the staggered firm entry into the Protocol as a change to the property rights associated with client relationships to study how advisors change their revenue model (commission vs. fee-based), obtain additional licensing, and increase investment in client relationships. Our setting has several attractive features for studying how property rights affect employee behavior. The financial advisory industry is highly regulated and provides information on registered advisors through the Financial Industry Regulatory Authority's (FINRA) BrokerCheck website4and the Securities and Exchange Commission's (SEC) Investment Adviser Public Disclosure (IAPD) database, which allow us to carefully track individual financial advisors across firms, locations, and time.5We have detailed information on when advisors obtain licensing to sell different types of financial products and details on disputes with their clients. Because firms join the Protocol at various times, we observe changes in the ownership of client relationships from firm-owned to advisor-owned for some (but not all) advisors over time. The richness of the data allows us to not only control for observable characteristics, but also use high-dimensional fixed effects to control for unobservable heterogeneity across time, geography, regulatory regime, firms, and individuals (and their interactions). Importantly, individual advisors competing in the same geographic area at the same time may have different rights to their client relationship due to firm-level decisions that occur far away and affect thousands of other advisors with the same firm.
Consider the example of two advisors working in Raleigh, NC, where one works for UBS and one works for Edward Jones. Their offices are less than 400 feet away from each other overlooking the 3700 block of Glenwood Avenue. Each is positioned less than three miles from the prestigious Carolina Country Club, where they likely compete for the same clients. In 2004, executives in UBS's corporate offices in New York decided to join the Protocol, while executives in Edward Jones's St. Louis office did not (and still have not done so by the end of our sample). We examine how the exogenous (to the individual advisor) change in property rights affects the UBS advisor's behavior relative to that of the Edward Jones advisor. For instance, does the UBS advisor treat the client relationship differently and improve service quality now that she effectively owns the relationship? Does she invest more in the relationship and shift from a short-term, commission-based revenue model to a longer term fee-based model? And does the shift in ownership lead her to seek additional client-related licensing?
After confirming that the Protocol increases advisor mobility between Protocol firms, as expected, we begin by documenting how advisors' model for generating revenues from their client relationships changes. The vast majority (over 92%) of financial advisors begin their careers as brokers, which allows them to sell commission-based products but not to offer fee-based advice. We find that after an employee is covered by the Protocol, she is 17.8% more likely to register as an investment adviser, which allows her to provide fee-based advice, relative to the unconditional benchmark after controlling for factors such as local market conditions and firm quality through county-year and firm fixed effects. Given that employee turnover is inherently associated with the Protocol and the opportunity for sorting, throughout our paper we focus on within-employee changes. To further rule out the possibility of assortative matching effects associated with advisors choosing to work at the types of firms that join the Protocol, we employ job spell fixed effects that exploit variation within the firm-employee pair (i.e., job spell). We find an increase in the decision to become an investment adviser even among those financial advisors who join a non-Protocol firm but are thrust into the Protocol over the course of their employment with the firm.
Although the founding firms were motivated by legal liability, firm-level selection into the Protocol could confound our interpretation, especially if the firm changes other policies at the time it enters the Protocol.6To address this concern, we exploit within-firm-year variation in the change in property rights that is due to geography. Some states, such as California, have historically not enforced nonsolicitation agreements. As such, we expect advisors in California to be less affected by the change in property rights due to Protocol membership. We find that among advisors working for the same firm at the same time, those located at branches in states where the courts have been more favorable to nonsolicitation agreements, have a higher likelihood of becoming an investment adviser following Protocol inclusion.
We also observe other ways in which advisors change their behavior in response to entry into Protocol. Financial advisors can acquire additional licenses over their careers that allow them to sell more specialized products to their customers or to take on a supervisory role within the firm. Using a similar empirical strategy as before, we find that after exposure to the Protocol, advisors are more likely to obtain additional client-facing sales licenses and less likely to obtain supervisory licenses. These results suggest that the change in ownership of the client relationship affects advisors' licensing decisions over the course of their career. The acquisition of licenses that are more client facing, and therefore more valuable to the relationship, increase, while the relative benefits from being promoted within the firm and engaging in managerial tasks decline.
We next examine how exposure to the Protocol affects advisors' treatment of their clients. Clients who use an advisor often lack financial sophistication (Foerster et al. (2017,2018)). The credence good nature of financial advice makes it difficult for clients to evaluate whether advisors are providing good advice or are instead being swayed by short-term compensation incentives, such as kickbacks or commissions. An advisor that fears being held up by the firm may be more willing to place a client in an unsuitable investment and receive higher commissions up front versus investing in the relationship even if doing so may generate surplus that can be captured through fee-based advice (Inderst and Ottaviani (2012)). If advisors are allowed to retain the client relationship upon leaving the firm, advisors are likely to have less incentive to engage in myopic behavior and should be more likely to invest in the client relationship. Consistent with this hypothesis, we find that after the advisor is covered by the Protocol, their incidence of client disputes drops by 20.3%. Without ownership of the client relationship, an advisor's own financial interests are not as well aligned with those of the client.
Turning to advisor outcomes, we find that advisors are more likely to make theBarron'sTop Advisor list, have more client assets under management, and are more likely to start their own Registered Investment Advisor (RIA) firms after being covered by the Protocol. These results suggest that the right to client relationships appears to affect advisors' incentives to provide high-quality service, grow the book of business, and even consider entrepreneurial pursuits where the advisor can fully capture all the revenue that the relationship assets generate.
Our paper contributes to the emerging literature on the financial advisory industry. Egan, Matvos, and Seru (2019b) show that a significant percentage of advisors engage in misconduct against their clients and suggest that certain firms “specialize” in misconduct and cater to unsophisticated consumers. Misconduct among financial advisors has been shown to be related to peer effects, regulators, gender discrimination, financial pressure, and early life background.7In a contemporaneous working paper that also examines events around the Protocol, Gurun, Stoffman, and Yonker (2020) focus primarily on firm-level effects of entering the Protocol and find that firms that enter the Protocol change their recruiting strategies.8In our paper, we focus largely on individual employee-level effects of the Protocol and document that advisors' treatment of clients is related to the incentives created by the assignment of property rights to the client relationship. We find that a governance-based policy (the Protocol) improves service quality and thus has significant welfare effects for investors. Further, despite licensing being pervasive in the industry, we are the first to examine the determinants of advisors' licensing choices.
We contribute to the literature on property rights, ownership, and firm boundaries. Simester and Wernerfelt (2005) study ownership of productive assets in the carpentry trade to evaluate the allocation of asset ownership between an employer and his employees. They find that employees are generally more careful with their own tools (the productive asset) than with the tools owned by the firm. Baker and Hubbard (2004) exploit the introduction of on-board computers in the trucking industry as a shock to noncontractible care of the productive asset to examine its effect on ownership. Massa, Reuter, and Zitzewitz (2010) show that mutual fund families weigh the benefits of naming managers (which, they argue, gives the manager “ownership” of the fund's record) against the cost associated with their increased future bargaining power. Our paper differs in that we examine the change in property rights to a given asset between economic actors, which allows us to shed light on changes in the extent to which employees care for their productive assets (client relationships).
Finally, we contribute to a growing literature on effects of labor mobility on employees and firms. A recent survey finds that nearly 40% of the workforce had agreed to a noncompete at some point during their career (Prescott, Bishara, and Starr (2016)). Many papers rely on geographic differences in the enforceability of noncompete agreements (and, in some cases, time-series variation in enforceability due to judicial decisions) as a source of variation in mobility in an attempt to establish a causal relationship.9Most related to our study, Jeffers (2020) and Lavetti, Simon, and White (2020) find that while noncompete contracts reduce labor mobility, they are also associated with an increase in investment by the firm, suggesting that reducing labor mobility may reduce the firm's hold-up problem. Unlike existing literature that focuses largely on the impact of state-level variation in noncompete enforceability, in our setting the source of variation is at the firm level and varies over time, which allows us to observe two advisors at the same location at the same point in time but with different levels of mobility. Another important difference is that these papers focus on investment by the firm, whereas we focus on investment by the employee. Our results suggest that an employee's fear of being held up by the firm is an additional cost of mobility restrictions.
This paper is organized as follows. SectionIprovides background on the investment advisory industry and the Protocol for Broker Recruiting. SectionIIdescribes the data used in the study. SectionIIIexamines licensing. SectionIVexamines client outcomes, and SectionVexamines advisor outcomes. SectionVIdocuments robustness of the main findings. Finally, SectionVIIconcludes.
article.I. Investment Advisory Industry:
Investment advisory firms are important financial institutions that affect the well-being of many households. Hung et al. (2008) report that the majority of individual investors use an advisor for investment decisions (outside of employer-sponsored retirement plans). In 2019, advisory firms employed over 600,000 individuals, generated more than $34 billion in revenues, and managed trillions in assets.10An important reason the industry is so large is that many households have a low level of financial literacy (Lusardi and Mitchell (2007)) and as a result must rely on advisors.
Gennaioli, Shleifer, and Vishny (2015) argue that financial advice is a trust-based service, similar to medicine. Just as patients trust their doctors and do not want to go to a random doctor, even if equally qualified, clients of financial advisors may become similarly attached. Although the questions we address apply more generally to all firms that provide trust-based relationship services, we use the financial advisory industry as our empirical setting.
A key feature of advisory relationships is that the client relationship is inalienable from the advisor. Moreover, the firm cannot write a complete contract on these relationships, for example, by offering bonuses for playing golf with clients at least “X” times a year or for learning the names of at least two of the client's children. First, the value of different actions varies across advisor-client pairs (e.g., developing a personal relationship, investing in persuasive advertising, and networking to friends and colleagues. (Gennaioli, Shleifer, and Vishny (2015)). Second, it takes time for an advisor to build trust with a client (Foerster et al. (2017)), and the payoff to the advisor of doing so may accrue over the lifetime of the account.
The role of the advisor-client relationship is central to the revenue-generating process of the advisory firm. A typical wirehouse advisor is paid a contract that is linear in the annual revenue they generate for the firm (e.g., 40% of commission and fees generated). A commission-based model allows the advisor to capture the present value of their fees. Under a fee-based model, in contrast, the advisor charges the client an annual fee (typically around 1% of assets under management) for constructing and maintaining the client's investment portfolio and providing general advice. In the latter case, commissions are not charged for each portfolio transaction and advisors have little incentive to “churn” the portfolio; the advisor's fee will grow accordingly rather, as the client's account grows in value over time. Advisors therefore trade off larger up-front commissions with a growing stream of cash flows over the life of the relationship. An advisor with hold-up concerns may have an incentive to focus on short-term, commission-based transactions. The recommendations they make may be biased toward the advisor's interests (not the client's interests) because commissions are not paid by the customers but rather by the providers of financial products. Indeed, Inderst and Ottaviani (2012) develop a model that suggests financial advisors may choose to engage in commission-based business with naïve clients even if investing in a relationship by recommending the most suitable products for a customer creates more total surplus. In our sample, only one in three advisors register as an investment adviser, which allows them to offer fee-based advice, in spite of the fact that commission-based advisors are twice as likely to be fired by their clients.11This further suggests that concerns about future hold-up problems may affect advisors' treatment of their customers.
Advisors who have a demonstrated ability to gain client trust and attract assets are in high demand in the competitive advisory industry. Firms often seek to recruit top advisors from their current employer with large signing bonuses. Nonsolicitation agreements and a fiduciary duty to the current employer prevent a departing advisor from notifying her clients ahead of an upcoming move. As soon as the advisor resigns, the firm from which she departed typically assigns other advisors or branch managers to call her former clients and persuade them to remain with the firm. The departing advisor is unable to solicit her former clients and in most cases can talk with them only if the clients seek her out and find her new contact information.12We provide several examples of nonsolicitation clauses inInternet AppendixTableIA.I.13
To mitigate the poaching of their employees, incumbent firms often seek temporary restraining orders and sue departing advisors for breach of employment contract related to the solicitation of clients. In contrast to noncompete agreements, which prohibit a former employee from competing against a former employer for a specified amount of time, nonsolicitation agreements are more narrowly aimed at preventing an employee from soliciting his former employer's clients. Courts have generally ruled more favorably on enforcing nonsolicitation agreements compared to noncompete agreements as nonsolicitations impose fewer limitations on an employee's right to work.
In 2000, the SEC adopted Regulation S-P, which implements privacy rules declared under section 504 of the Gramm-Leach-Bliley Act. The Act required that financial institutions establish standards to protect customer information and prohibited financial institutions from sharing a client's private information with other financial firms without the client's consent. Because poaching firms (with the assistance of the departing advisor) would typically prepare account-transfer documents that included account information and Social Security numbers ahead of advisors' transitions, there was a concern that the practice of broker poaching generated violations of the Act.
Exacerbating this problem for New York-based firms was the fact that Eliot Spitzer, New York Attorney General at the time, had started to use the Martin Act, a powerful law unique to New York state, to target financial firms. After reaching settlements for the late-trading scandal and conflicts of interest in investment banking research, Wall Street firms began to actively evaluate business units for exposure to legal liability and identified wealth management as a possible concern.14Additionally, competing firms often fought in court over whether advisors were free to continue serving existing clients after transitioning between firms. Such disputes, in which client relationships were treated like firm property, enriched attorneys but undermined the client-focused image that the financial advisory firms tried to cultivate. Although mitigating legal costs and liability for poaching brokers was important to firms, no firm had an incentive to unilaterally end the practice. Change to existing patterns would therefore require some form of coordination among firms.
On August 19, 2004, three of the largest New York brokerage firms—Citigroup Global Markets (Smith Barney), UBS Financial Services, and Merrill Lynch, Pierce, Fenner & Smith—entered into an agreement called The Protocol for Broker Recruiting (Protocol). The Protocol waived employee nonsolicitation agreements among member firms, thereby shifting the property rights associated with the client relationship from the firm to the advisor, and increased labor mobility, as it allowed advisors to transfer from one Protocol firm to another without fear of legal recourse.
In entering into this agreement, the firms standardized what client data could be taken to a new firm and agreed not to bring legal action against other members of the Protocol, thereby minimizing litigation risk. Any financial advisory firm can enter and exit The Protocol on short notice and at low cost. The Protocol allows departing advisors to take five pieces of client data—name, address, phone number, e-mail address, and account titles—and to solicit these existing clients (but not other clients of their former firm).15The full text of the Protocol is provided in theInternet Appendix. The Protocol has been subject to legal challenges, and the courts have consistently held member firms to the terms of the Protocol. Importantly, non-Protocol advisors moving to Protocol firms (or Protocol advisors moving to non-Protocol firms) do not receive similar treatment.16
Our empirical objective is to identify how a change in property rights associated with client relationships affects employee behavior. Advisors have a large degree of freedom in terms of deciding what licenses to pursue or whether to offer commission or fee-based advice. Of course, the correlation between these choices and whether an advisor has property rights under The Protocol could be confounded by a number of factors, such as the advisor's inherent quality or “type.” To observe the effect of property rights, an ideal experiment would randomly assign client relationship property rights to advisors. Absent such an experiment, we rely on the change in property rights among advisors working for firms that choose to enter into the Protocol. The central concern regarding causality under our approach is that financial advisory firms choose to enter the Protocol and thus financial advisors are not randomly allocated into this treatment, but rather are selected based on the firm they chose to work for.
The granular nature of our data offers several key advantages that help us address the endogenous entry of firms into the Protocol. Most employees work for large firms that are unlikely to shift firm-wide policies for an individual employee. For each employee-year observation in our data, we have a unique identifier for the employee, the firm they work for, and the county in which the employee works. This information allows us to include employee, firm, county×year, and job spell fixed effects in our analysis. Employee fixed effects remove time-invariant advisor characteristics, including their propensity to invest in licensing, ability, educational background, and gender. Firm fixed effects remove time-invariant firm characteristics, such as average employee quality and the firm's tendency to invest in licensing or generate customer disputes. By conditioning out county×year effects, we control for time-varying changes in the regulatory environment as well as differences in local labor markets and economic conditions over time.17Our setting therefore allows us to compare different individuals (some of whom work for a Protocol firm and some of whom do not) that work in thesamecounty at thesametime and therefore face similar economic incentives to invest in licensing or take advantage of their customers.
The job spell fixed effects (employee×firm) control for both time-invariant characteristics of the employee-firm pair and assortative matching between employees and firms based on Protocol status. In these tests, the effect of The Protocol is estimated only on those advisors who join a firm before it enters the Protocol and remain with it after the firm enters the agreement.
Although the fixed effects above rule out many alternative explanations, a remaining concern is that firms may choose to join the Protocol at the same time they change other firm policies that could affect employee behavior. For example, if a firm changes the compensation scheme for fee-based advice at the time it joins the Protocol, then it may be the case that an advisor chooses to become an investment adviser for reasons unrelated to the change in property rights. To address this concern, we rely on the fact that many of the employees in our data work for the same firm at the same time but are geographically dispersed throughout the country. As such, we expect employees to show variation in their response to the change in property rights because of differences in state-level enforcement of nonsolicitation agreements. For example, an advisor that works in California, a state that tends to view labor mobility restrictions less favorably, is less likely to be affected than an advisor working for the same firm at the same time in Texas. These within-firm-year tests can help isolate the effect of changes in property rights due to the Protocol from other confounding time-varying firm decisions.
article.II. Data and Summary Statistics:
We obtain data on signatories to the Protocol from the website maintained by Carlile, Patchen, & Murphy LLP.18The website lists the firm's legal name, contact person information, the date the firm joined The Protocol, and the date the firm withdrew (if any). Firms are listed if they were a signatory at any point in time. We are able to match the legal names from FINRA Form BD and SEC Form ADV filings to obtain Central Registration Depository (CRD) numbers for signatories. To match ambiguous cases, we use the individual registrations of contact persons or firm phone numbers and e-mail domains. In TableI, we report the names and dates of the large firms (>1,000 advisors) that join the Protocol during our sample period from January 1, 1999 to January 1, 2020. In Figure1, we plot the growth in Protocol membership over our sample period. When the founding members (UBS, Merrill Lynch, and Smith Barney) joined in 2004, approximately 10% of the advisors in the industry became subject to the Protocol. Other large firms, Morgan Stanley, Wachovia, and Raymond James, followed in 2006, and Protocol coverage grew steadily thereafter. By the end of our sample, one out of every three advisors in the industry was under the Protocol. Growth in the latter part of the sample is due largely to the influx of smaller firms and RIAs. Despite the ease and low cost of entering the Protocol, however, several large and notable firms, such as Edward Jones and Goldman Sachs, have never joined.
Although withdrawal from the Protocol is permitted and low cost (requiring only 10 days notice), less than 7% of firms have withdrawn from the Protocol. These firms were generally small, but two large firms exited the Protocol at the end of 2017—Morgan Stanley and UBS. One concern voiced among large wirehouses was that advisors were using the cover afforded by the Protocol to exit and start their own firms.19Both Morgan Stanley and UBS pointed to this unintended consequence of The Protocol in explaining their exit. A prediction of our study is that while exiting the Protocol may help limit labor mobility, it may also reduce advisors' incentives to invest in long-term client relationships. The spike in departures from these firms in the 10-day exit window is consistent with advisors valuing the mobility that The Protocol provides. Consistent with the change in the ownership of the client relationship back to the firm also affecting the firm's incentive to invest in client relationships, Morgan Stanley's press release announcing their departure mentioned increased investment on part of the firm: “This requires a meaningful investment in our existing, exceptional talent and delivery of additional resources, capabilities and intellectual capital that will enhance performance and deepen client relationships.”20
In the United States, all registered representatives (the legal term for what we refer to as “brokers”) must be registered with FINRA by their employer using Form U4. During our 1999–2020 sample period, the vast majority of financial advisors registered with FINRA. Financial advisors may also registered as an investment adviser with the SEC. About half of brokers also register as investment advisers. In less than 10% of advisor-year observations, a financial advisor registered only as an investment adviser and not as a broker; many of these observations are of formerly registered brokers. The data on brokers come from FINRA Form U4, which provides detailed information on advisors' characteristics, employment histories, licensing, and history of customer disputes and other misconduct. This form must be updated following any material change in the advisor's information (including change in employment or disclosure of misconduct). FINRA and state regulators jointly operate the CRD, which serves as a repository for Form U4 and related regulatory filings. Importantly, the CRD assigns each advisor and firm a unique identification number that is constant over time, which allows us to carefully track employment movements. FINRA shares much of this information with the public via its BrokerCheck website. The SEC provides similar information for investment advisers through the IAPD website, as reporting was harmonized under Dodd-Frank.
We build a data set of financial advisors from disclosures available through BrokerCheck and the IAPD website. We use these disclosures to create an annual panel data set that provides information about each individual advisor, including their employer, the location of the branch where the employer works, and information about their licensing and customer complaints, over the period 1999–2020.21Our estimation sample contains data on nearly one million financial advisors and over 9,000 firms. We summarize key variables from the data in TableII.
In our paper, a key set of dependent variables come from additional licensing the advisor acquires over the course of their career. Advisors must take a series of exams that are standardized across the industry to sell securities to clients or to supervise other financial advisors. In Panel A of TableII, we list the most common licenses in our data. Advisors who are new to the profession typically take the Series 6 exam or the Series 7 securities exam. The Series 6 only allows the advisor to sell securities that are accompanied by a prospectus (e.g., mutual funds). It is common to see the Series 6 exam taken by advisors at insurance-focused employers such as State Farm or Northwestern Mutual. The Series 7 subsumes all of the securities that are allowed in the Series 6 and also allows the advisor to sell individual securities (e.g., stocks). Further, advisors must take the Series 63 or Series 66 exam as a prerequisite for being licensed by a state.22In our sample, 38.9% of advisors have a Series 6 license, whereas 64.8% have a Series 7. The modal set of licenses for an advisor in our sample is a Series 7 and Series 63, with the Series 6 and Series 63 being a close second. Conditional on passing these exams, the median advisor earns these basic licenses in their first year of employment.
As the advisor's career progresses, they may seek additional licensing. For example, advisors wishing to transition from (or in addition to) a commission-based approach to an annual fee-based model must pass the Series 65 or Series 66 exam and register with the SEC or their state regulator. Advisors may wish to sell additional securities to their clients who are not covered under a Series 7 licenses. For example, an advisor must pass the Series 3 or Series 31 exam if they want to sell futures to the client. In our sample, 33.9% of advisors become investment advisers, whereas 23.2% have earned additional sales licenses. These investments in licensing tend to be taken later in the advisor's career. Conditional on earning the Series 65, the median Series 65 exam is passed in the advisor's sixth year of their career.
Over the course of their careers, advisors may transition into a managerial role within the branch. Branch managers are tasked with disciplining advisors, approving new accounts and trades, and determining the appropriateness of investment recommendations. To do so, these advisors must pass exams such as the Series 24 or Series 26 principal's exam.23In our sample, 13.4% of advisors have a Series 24 license, whereas 4.6% have a Series 26 license.
In addition to examining how the Protocol affects licensing, we also study how the Protocol affects client and advisor outcomes. We do not have data on individual account performance. As such, we proxy for client outcomes using customer disputes as in Charoenwong, Kwan, and Umar (2019). Customer disputes are infrequent in our sample, occurring in less than 1% of advisor-years. We report summary statistics at the advisor and advisor-year levels in Panel B of TableII. We also observe whether the advisor was named to theBarron'sTop Advisor List.Barron'shas published a list of the top financial advisors in the United States since 2004.Barron'sbases their ranking on the amount of assets under management, type of assets, growth of an advisor's practice, client retention, advanced degrees and industry designations, the size and shape of their teams, charitable and philanthropic work, and compliance records. In our sample, being named a top advisor is rare, with only one in 500 advisors ever making the list and the likelihood of making this list in any given year just 0.10%. Using data from Form ADV, we observe which advisors start their own RIA. Successful financial advisors may do so to capture a greater share of the revenue they generate. At a wirehouse firm, an advisor may receive less than half the revenue, with the rest going to the firm (which also covers costs). In a given year, the likelihood that an advisor leaves to start their own firm is just 0.08%.
Finally, to provide a general reference for career length and labor mobility, in Panel C of TableIIwe show that the average advisor works in the industry for 15 years for two different firms, spending over eight years at each.
If the Protocol reduces the costs to change firms, we expect to see increased movement of advisors after entry into the Protocol. The effect should be particularly strong in the first year after the firm joins, as advisors who were marginally unwilling to move prior to inclusion will view moving as more attractive given the reduction in transition costs. If labor market reshuffling occurs quickly, we would expect the observed movement to decline in subsequent years in the Protocol even though the threat of mobility remains high.
In addition to firm-to-firm movement, we find that The Protocol affects the likelihood of an advisor staying in the industry after separating from their firm. InInternet AppendixTableIA.II, we look at employee separations and find that advisors who are separated from Protocol firms are more likely to stay in the industry. Conditional on finding a new job, advisors covered by the Protocol have shorter unemployment spells than advisors leaving Protocol firms, even after separating due to employee misconduct. We view higher employment and shorter unemployment as evidence that advisors who can solicit their clients after they depart are more valuable in the labor market.24
We also estimate labor mobility models at the firm level. The results, reported inInternet AppendixTableIA.III, indicate that after joining the Protocol, firms are more likely to poach advisors (and have advisors poached) from fellow Protocol members and less likely to recruit from firms outside of the Protocol. We find that overall employee headcount rises following the Protocol, consistent with the claim in Gurun, Stoffman, and Yonker (2020) that firms join the Protocol when they are actively looking to grow their firm. Although firm-level tests such as these should be interpreted with caution given that the choice to join the Protocol is endogenous to the firm, they are consistent with the view that Protocol inclusion is related to changes in a firm's employment practices.
Allowing employees to take their book of business with them should they choose to depart may attract higher quality employees ex ante. For a small sample of the advisors in our data, we have Uniform Combined State Law Examination scores from when they entered the industry.25InInternet AppendixTableIA.IV, we regress exam score performance on Protocol status. Using exam performance as a proxy for new-employee quality, we find that Protocol status and employee quality are positively related. In particular, we find that new employee exam scores and pass rates are higher, and the number of attempts to pass the exam lower, after a firm joins the Protocol. This suggests that when firms modify their contracting environment by changing their Protocol status, they affect labor market sorting given the types of candidates they can hire (Strobl and Van Wesep (2013)). Importantly, models of employee behavior that do not control for employee quality are difficult to interpret. As such, in the remainder of the paper we include employee fixed effects (and in some cases job spell fixed effects) to control for time-invariant employee quality.
article.III. Licensing:
In our setting, inclusion in the Protocol shifts the property rights to client relationships from the firm to the advisor. This change affects the advisor's incentives to engage with the client as it alleviates the hold-up problem and affects the advisor's value in the external labor market. In this section, we examine how advisors' professional licensing decisions change in response.
Licensing is pervasive in the financial advisory industry and determines the standard of care that advisors provide their clients, the products that advisors are allowed to provide, and advisors' ability to manage other employees. The presence and timing of these licenses are observable at the advisor-year level and the licenses are standardized across the industry. Given that The Protocol affects an advisors' ability to transfer existing clients, as opposed to starting over with a new book of business, it decreases the advisor's cost of leaving their employer and increases their value in the labor market for firms looking to poach client-facing talent. These changes yield several testable implications with regard to an advisor's licensing choices.
As we note in SectionII, hold-up concerns may affect advisors' recommendations. An advisor has an incentive to engage in transactional or commission-based business if he fears future hold-up problems.27A commission-based model incentivizes the advisor to “churn” the portfolio by trading more. By shifting the property rights to the client relationship, the Protocol allows us to examine how incomplete contracting in the financial advisory industry affects the nature of financial advice. We expect that after exposure to the Protocol, advisors invest in licensing that allows them to become fee-based advisers. This revenue generation model can better align advisor-client interests as in this case advisers have little incentive to trade excessively. Rather, the adviser's dollar fee each year will grow together with the client's account over time. We test this hypothesis using equation (2). The results are reported in TableIII.
We define dependent the variable,Investment Adviser, as an indicator based on whether the advisor is registered with the SEC to provide financial advice. In most cases, an investment adviser must pass the Series 65 exam or the combination of Series 66 and Series 7 exams before registering as an investment adviser. In some cases, the presence of a third-party certification such as Certified Financial Planner (CFP) or Chartered Financial Analyst (CFA) can substitute for the Series 65 exam.28
In column (1), we find strong evidence that employees increase their likelihood of becoming an investment adviser after their employer enters the Protocol. In particular, compared to the unconditional probability that an advisor is anInvestment Adviserin our sample is 33.9% in a given year, we find that the likelihood that an employee becomes anInvestment Adviserincreases by 17.8% (=0.06040.339) after being covered by the Protocol. As we include employee fixed effects, the coefficient on Protocol is estimated using variation only for those individuals for which Protocol status changes over their career. In addition to employee fixed effects, we control for past basic licensing (Series 6, Series 7, and Series 63), experience, and year×county fixed effects. Past licensing and experience control for employee behavior among advisors with similar credentials at the same point in their careers, as investments in client relationships can take longer to pay off over a longer time frame for advisors just starting their career relative to advisors near retirement. The year×county fixed effects control for time-varying local conditions that may influence incentives to become anInvestment Adviser. The results in column (1) thus imply that relative to an advisor with similar years experience and similar licensing who works in the same county during the same year, an advisor whose employer has joined the Protocol is more likely to become anInvestment Adviserthan prior to his employer joining the Protocol. In column (2), we add firm fixed effects to control for heterogeneity among firms that may influence employee behavior, such as differences in product offerings or training programs. Most large firms exhibit substantial within-firm variation on whether their employees choose to register as investment advisers, but some firms, especially smaller ones, may encourage all of their employees to adopt a specific business model. Adding firm fixed-effects helps control for this influence. This specification may be overly conservative, however, if The Protocol influences individuals' adoption of a particular business model and advisors sort ex post to firms that tend to employ that model. Nonetheless in column (2) we continue to find an increased likelihood of an advisor becoming anInvestment Adviserafter the firm joins the Protocol.
Another consideration due to sorting is that firms that change Protocol status may simultaneously change the types of candidates they can hire (Strobl and Van Wesep (2013)). To control for time-invariant employee quality and assortative matching between employees and firms, in column (3) we include job spell fixed effects. In these tests, the effect of The Protocol is estimated only on those advisors who join a firm before it enters the Protocol and remain with it after the firm enters the agreement, eliminating matching concerns based on contemporaneous Protocol status. Thus, all advisors with variation in Protocol status initially joined a non-Protocol firm and then were thrust into Protocol status due to a firm-level decision. These effects subsume individual and firm fixed effects.29We find that even among advisors who work for the same firm both before and after the firm joins the Protocol, these employees are more likely to become anInvestment Adviserafter the firm joins the Protocol (relative to similar advisors in the same area at the same time and at the same point in their career). We note that, this specification may be conservative as it only examines variation within a job spell, that is, it does not include observations for advisors who move after they become covered by The Protocol.
Advisors who take advantage of The Protocol to change employers may benefit more from the property rights they receive. Accordingly, we expect larger effects among movers than among those who remain with their original firm. In column (4), we test this conjecture by interactingInvestment AdviserwithChanged Firm. The interaction term is equal to 1 when an advisor changes from one Protocol firm to another and captures the incremental effect for advisors who change firms after the Protocol. Consistent with our earlier results, we find that all employees covered by the Protocol are more likely to register as anInvestment Adviser. However, employees who depart after they are covered by the Protocol are 67.6% (=0.03630.0537) more likely to become an investment adviser compared to those who stay at their initial Protocol firm. We note that this interaction does not identify a causal effect of moving, but rather suggests that the employees who chose to leave may have been more concerned about hold-up problems.
Although an individual at a large wirehourse may have little influence over her firm's Protocol status, afirm'sdecision to join the Protocol may be endogenous with respect to other firm decisions. For example, at the time they join the Protocol, firms could increase investment advisers' compensation arrangements. In this case, an individual registering as an investment adviser may be due to an increase in property rights or may be due to the change in the compensation scheme.
To address this issue, we examinewithin-firm-yearvariation in an employee's sensitivity to the Protocol. If changes in adviser licensing are related to other observed or unobserved changes in firm policies, we would expect a relatively homogeneous impact on all employees in the firm. If, however, the channel is based on the Protocol's impact on nonsolicitation agreements, we would expect the impact to be more (less) pronounced for those employees who work in states in which courts are more (less) likely to enforce these agreements.
The richness of our data allows us to compare advisors working for the same firm at the same time but at branches in states where the courts have viewed labor immobility contracts more versus less favorably. Courts in California, for example, have tended to have a less favorable view of nonsolicitation agreements (Gilson (1999)). One prominent explanation for state-level differences in nonsolicitation enforcement is the adoption of Field Codes at time of statehood.31Based on the legal origin of Field Codes, we setNonsolicit Stateto zero if the employee works at a branch outside of California, Montana, or North Dakota and to one otherwise.32
In column (5), we repeat our estimation approach but interactProtocolwithNonsolicit State. We control for the possibility of time-varying changes in firm policies by including firm×year fixed effects that subsume the main effect of The Protocol. The interaction coefficient therefore estimates the difference in the propensity to become an investment adviser for an employee with more sensitivity to Protocol status relative to an employee with less sensitivity that works for the same firm in the same year. Importantly, these tests control for the possibility that firm-level policy changes coincide with firm inclusion in the Protocol.
We find that relative to our estimate in column (1), advisors within a given firm and year (and therefore Protocol status) are 26.8% (=0.01620.0604) more likely to become an investment adviser if they work in a state that more strictly enforces nonsolicit clauses. Put differently, even though a Wells Fargo advisor in California is likely exposed to the same time-varying firm policies as a Wells Fargo advisor in Texas, the Texas advisor displays more sensitivity to their firm joining The Protocol as Texas was more likely to enforce nonsolicitation agreements before the Protocol.
Taken together, the results above suggest that when the firm shifts property rights to client accounts to the employee, the employee is more likely to become an investment adviser. Relative to their peers, as well as to their own observed behavior prior to the Protocol, individual financial advisors are more likely to shift a transaction-based to a fee-based revenue generation model by registering as an investment adviser after being covered by the Protocol.
Above we show that The Protocol can affect the nature of the client relationship by impacting the decision to become an investment adviser. The Protocol may also affect the scope of an advisor's relationship with their client and firm by impacting incentives to earn additional licenses. For example, an advisor may obtain a Series 3 license (Commodities and Futures) to better serve their client. Alternatively, the advisor may obtain a Series 24 license (General Securities Principal) to supervise the firm's employees. We hypothesize that following inclusion in the Protocol, advisors will trade off investment in other securities licenses: increasing investment in licenses that complement their ownership of the client asset and reducing investment in licenses not directly related to the client asset.
In TableIV, we repeat our empirical approach from TableIIIbut replace the dependent variable with eitherSales(Panel A), which is the number of all sales-related exams that an advisor passes,33andSupervise(Panel B), which is equal to 1 if an advisor obtains a supervisory license (Series 24 or 26). In addition, in these tests we include county×year×investment adviser fixed effects to account for any general effect (including time-varying ones) of being an investment adviser.
For reference, in column (1) of Panel A the average advisor in our sample has 0.232 additional sales licenses. We find that an advisor is more likely to add an additional sales license after being covered by the Protocol. As before, the fixed effects specification implies that this increase is relative to an advisor with the same experience working in the same county-year as well as to the advisor's own likelihood prior to his employer joining the Protocol. Our results are similar in column (2), where we include firm fixed effects to control for heterogeneity among firms that may influence employee behavior, and in column (3), where we include job spell fixed effects. In column (4), we find that much of this effect is driven by those advisors who changed firms. In column (5), we repeat the interaction onProtocolandNonsolicit State. We find that advisors working for the same firm at the same time but located in states where enforcement of nonsolicitation agreements is strong are more likely to add additional sales licenses. In Figure3(Panel B), we observe a similar trend in sales licensing prior to joining Protocol, and an abrupt diverging trend after an individual is covered by Protocol.
Within the same UBS branch in 2005, some individuals responded to the change in property rights by investing in the client relationship and registering as an investment adviser while others did not. We expect that within the branch those who become an investment adviser are more likely to invest in the client relationship and add additional client-facing licenses, all else equal. In column (6), we add an additional within-firm-year test based on whether the advisor registers as an investment adviser. We find that relative to the baseline in column (1), individuals who are exposed to the Protocol and register as an investment adviser are substantially more likely to add an additional sales license than those who do not, even though both individuals are exposed to the same time-varying firm policies and local market conditions that may influence this choice. Although the estimate does not constitute a causal relationship, it does suggest that the individuals who decide to shift toward a fee-based model also are the same individuals who obtain additional licenses to serve their client.
TableIV, Panel B, reports results for supervisor licenses. We find that advisors substitute investment away from supervisor licenses following the Protocol. For reference, the unconditional probability ofSuperviseis 18.13%. An advisor is 9.4% (=−0.01710.1813) less likely to add a license that allows them to supervise others after entering the Protocol. Our results are similar in column (2), where we include firm fixed effects, and in column (3), where we include job spell fixed effects. In column (4), we find that advisors who subsequently change firms are even less likely to become supervisors than advisors who stay at the same firm. In Figure3(Panel C), we find an abrupt and negative change in the likelihood that an advisor acquires a supervisor license after being covered by Protocol.
Turning to our within-firm-year analysis, in column (5) we repeat the interaction betweenProtocolandNonsolicit Stateand find that the effect onSupervisory Licensesis negative for advisors working in states that strongly enforce nonsolicitation agreements, although the effect is economically modest and not statistically different from zero. In column (6), we show that individuals registered as an investment adviser are less likely to become supervisors than those financial advisors who do not become an investment advisor.34
Taken together, our results suggest that advisors respond to The Protocol by shifting investment toward licensing that complements ownership of the asset and away from licensing that is less related to the client asset.
article.IV. Client Outcomes:
If financial advisors have de facto ownership of their clients under the Protocol, then advisors may have greater incentive to invest in the long-term relationship asset. Although engaging in portfolio churning, unauthorized trades, or opportunistic behavior could boost compensation in the short run, doing so may risk damaging longer term relationships with clients. On the other hand, allowing clients to move with an advisor could soften the labor market penalties to misconduct as documented by Egan, Matvos, and Seru (2019b)—a firm may be more willing to take a chance on a tarnished advisor if that advisor can bring a large and lucrative book of assets with them.
Given this tension, we examine whether customer disputes, which represent cases in which the relationship between advisor and client is damaged,35change after an advisor is covered by the Protocol (Charoenwong, Kwan, and Umar (2019)). Typical disputes include concerns related to investment suitability, excess fees, and portfolio churning, among others. We repeat the empirical setup of TableIII, replacing the dependent variable withCustomer Disputes, which takes a value of 1 if the advisor faces a customer complaint in a given year, and zero otherwise. The results are reported in TableV.36
We find that advisors take better care of client relationships after their firm enters the Protocol. Compared to the unconditional rate of an advisor generating a customer dispute in a given year is 0.79%, the likelihood of an employee generating a customer dispute decreases by 20.2% (=−0.00160.0079) after being covered by the Protocol. Recall that in this specification we control for experience and licensing and we include advisor and county-year fixed effects, so the reduction is relative to the advisor's own treatment of customers before beng covered by the Protocol as well as to contemporaneous behavior by other advisors within the same county, in the same year, with the same number of years in the profession.
Our results are similar if we add firm fixed effects (column (2)) and if we examine within job spells (column (3)). We find that much of the reduction in customer disputes occurs among advisors who changed firms, relative to advisors who are exposed to the Protocol but do not change firms (column (4)). In columns (5) and (6), we repeat our within-firm-year tests and find that the effect of The Protocol on customer disputes is stronger for individuals in states that enforce nonsolicitation agreements and for individuals who become registered investment advisers. These results suggest that the Protocol itself, and not changes to firm-level policies, affect advisor behavior. Taken together, our results support the view that not only do advisors invest in additional licensing when they have greater property rights, but they also invest more in the productive asset, that is, in client relationships.
article.V. Advisor Outcomes:
We next consider advisor outcomes besides licensing. If financial advisors are allowed to retain their clients if they leave a firm, then advisors may have greater incentive to invest in growing their business, even at their current firm, and are likely to invest in the relationship asset in ways that may not be captured by the customer dispute measure. Because client relationships are multifaceted, we employ a number of proxies for the growth and quality of client relationships.
Beginning in 2004,Barron'sbegan to publish a list of the top financial advisors in the United States.37Barron'sbases their ranking on the amount of assets an advisor manages, the type of assets, growth of an advisor's practice, client retention, advanced degrees and industry designations, the size and shape of their teams, their charitable and philanthropic work, and compliance records. Gerken and Momeni (2020) provide evidence that investors value and respond to theseBarron'srankings.
In TableVI, column (1), we employ a similar empirical approach as earlier models but change the dependent variable to an indicator for whether the advisor is on theBarron'slist in a given year. We find strong evidence that advisors are more likely to make theBarron'slist after their firm enters the Protocol. The unconditional probability that an advisor makes theBarron'slist is 0.10% in a given year. In column (1), we find that this likelihood increases by 70% (=0.00070.0010) after being covered by the Protocol. Given the fixed effect specification, this increase is relative to other investment advisers working in the same location at the same time, the firm-level average likelihood of making the list, and the advisor's own likelihood prior to joining the Protocol.
As an alternative way to examine advisor productivity, we obtain a cross-section of individual advisor-level assets under management from data provider Meridian IQ for 2013. The advantage of this data set is that we have granular data on the size of client assets for a large cross-section of advisors (not just the top advisors whoBarron'shighlights). The obvious disadvantage is that with a single cross-section we cannot employ the same specifications as we do with the panel data and thus the results must be interpreted with that caveat in mind. InInternet AppendixTableIA.VIII, we find thatProtocoladvisors have significantly more assets under management (25.2%=17.2768.5) even after controlling for licensing, time, geographic location, and investment adviser status.
In the later part of our sample, the increase in the number of firms joining the Protocol was driven in large part by wirehouse advisors of larger Protocol firms starting up their own registered investment adviser (RIA) firm. The newly created RIA would then join the Protocol, allowing the former wirehouse advisor to transfer existing clients to the new firm. Unlike the compensation scheme at the large wirehouse, however, the advisor could retain nearly all of the revenue generated from the client (while also bearing all the costs). This represents a complete transfer of ownership of the client asset. In column (2), we define a new firm dummy as equal to 1 if the advisor starts a new RIA (defined as an owner or director based on Form ADV filings). The unconditional probability of an advisor starting an RIA in a given year is low at 0.08%. In column (2), we find that the likelihood that an employee starts an RIA increases more than 37% (=0.00030.0008) after entering the Protocol. This increase is again relative to other investment advisers working in the same location at the same time, the firm-level average likelihood of starting an RIA, and the advisor's own likelihood prior to joining the Protocol.
Taken together, these results suggest that ownership of client relationships impacts productivity and other ways in which advisors manage their business. After coming under The Protocol, advisors are more likely to receive industry accolades, to increase the assets they manage, and to start new firms and take their clients with them.
article.VI. Robustness:
In this section, we discuss results of a number of robustness checks on the relationships between Protocol, licensing, and customer disputes. Our results are reported in theInternet Appendix.
A challenge to a casual interpretation of the results is related to our assumption that while firms choose to enter the Protocol, employees do not affect this choice. In the early years of the Protocol, the large wirehouse firms that joined employed thousands of employees, and thus it is plausible to believe that individual advisors had little influence over whether their firm joined the Protocol. In recent years, however, a number of smaller firms have joined for which this assumption may not hold. Indeed, an increasingly common practice is for advisors to use the Protocol to break away from their large wirehouse firm and bring their clients to a newly founded RIA. Thus, to avoid potential confounding effects in small firms where individual advisors may have more influence, we re-estimate column (1) from our licensing and customer dispute tables on the sub sample of advisors who work for firms with more than 100 employees. We report these results inInternet AppendixTableIA.IX. Our results using this large firm sample are similar, suggesting that it is the exogenous change in Protocol status and not potential endogenous advisor selection from small firms that drives our results.
In addition, the advent of the Protocol was likely less anticipated among those advisors working for the original adopters than among those advisors who work for firms that joined the Protocol during the later part of the sample. Although our specifications contain individual advisor and firm fixed effects, one might be concerned that early or late adoptees of The Protocol might have joined The Protocol for different reasons. InInternet AppendixTableIA.XIV, we interact Protocol with an indicator variable for the three founding members. Although the magnitude of the interaction effect is larger for advisors exposed to the original change in property rights, these differences are not statistically significant. Rather, the main effect of The Protocol on licensing and customer disputes is similar to our main findings, suggesting that our results are not driven by the original members.
To control for the types of firms that may enter the Protocol, in our previous analysis we include firm fixed effects to pull out common firm variation. One limitation of firm fixed effects is that the subject firms change over time. Indeed, mergers were common in this industry, with the result that the postmerger firm is not the same as before the merger, especially in the case of bank mergers during the financial crisis (e.g., J.P. Morgan and Bear Stearns or Merrill Lynch and Bank of America). InInternet AppendixTableIA.X, we remove all advisor-year observations for advisors who have experienced a merger. The results are similar.38
The BrokerCheck website provides information on advisors who are currently registered or have been registered within the last 10 years. It also contains records for any advisor whose registration with FINRA or a national securities exchange was barred more than 10 years ago. The data for this analysis were initially downloaded from the FINRA BrokerCheck website in 2017; we have since downloaded the data up to 2020. Given that the Protocol was established more than 10 years before our initial collection date, an advisor that exited the industry with a clean record in 2015 is in our data while an advisor that exited with a clean record in 2005 is not.
Given this structure, survivorship bias could arise if the relationship between The Protocol and our main variables of interest (licensing and customer disputes) differs for those individuals for whom we do not observe records, namely, advisors who exited the industry but were not terminated for bad behavior. Ideally, we would recover all missing records and re-estimate our models with the nonsurvivors. Although we do not observe nonsurvivors before our original sample, we can take advantage of the fact that we have downloaded the Brokercheck database multiple times. In particular, we can compare the records from the 2020 vintage and the earlier 2017 vintage to identify nonsurvivors from the 2017 vintage. InInternet AppendixTableIA.XII, we show that our results are similar using only the nonsurvivors from the 2017 vintage. These results suggest that survivorship bias is unlikely to be a concern in our setting.
article.VII. Conclusion:
In this paper, we exploit staggered firm-level entry into an industry agreement that waived existing nonsolicitation contracts in an effort to reduce litigation risk in the financial advisory industry. In doing so, member firms shifted property rights to client relationships from the firm to the employee. Inclusion in the Protocol increases employee labor mobility within the industry. Importantly, it also alters the employees' investment in industry licensing and client relationships. We find that advisors at Protocol firms acquire additional industry licenses and shift their business from a shorter term, commission-based model to a longer term, fee-based model. Consistent with this shift in business model, we show that these advisors take better care of their client relationships as evidenced by a drop in the rate of customer disputes upon their employer joining the Protocol. Our ability to use job spell, time, experience, firm, and geography-year fixed effects allows us to rule out alternative explanations such as industry trends, selection bias, and local economic effects. Our findings provide empirical support for property rights-based investment theories of human capital and document an offsetting cost to restricting labor mobility.
Our findings shed light on the recent policy debate that has proposed fiduciary standards on advisors to act in the best interest of clients or else face penalties. We examine an alternative governance-based mechanism to encourage alignment of interests and improve service quality to clients. By more closely linking the advisor to the client, the Protocol has led self-interested advisors to act more in the client's best interest. The results suggest that regulation without similar incentives may prove inadequate.
Editors: Stefan Nagel, Philip Bond, Amit Seru, and Wei Xiong
